content.content:
1 import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; 2 3 export const hvmPhases: Phase[] = [ 4 { 5 id: "what", 6 heading: "What Hivemind is", 7 body: ( 8 <> 9

10 Hivemind is strategic analytical software for operators reasoning under 11 uncertainty — founders, analysts, and decision-makers who have to commit 12 to a course of action when the evidence is incomplete and the stakes are 13 real. 14

15

16 The one-line pitch: instead of a memo full of confident-sounding prose, 17 you get a structured argument you can defend, attack, and update — with 18 the assumptions named and the evidence attached. 19

20 21 ), 22 }, 23 { 24 id: "problem", 25 heading: "What problem it solves", 26 body: ( 27 <> 28

29 Most strategic deliberation fails the same way. A team writes a memo, 30 fluent prose hides the load-bearing assumptions, and nobody can argue 31 with it because there is nothing concrete to argue against. The team 32 commits anyway, and when reality disagrees, no one can point at the 33 step where the reasoning broke. 34

35

36 Hivemind targets that failure. The working artifact is not a paragraph 37 — it is a tree of claims with sources attached and a confidence number 38 on each. When the answer changes, you can point at the claim that 39 moved, and so can the next person who picks up the file. 40

41 42 ), 43 }, 44 { 45 id: "how", 46 heading: "How it works, briefly", 47 body: ( 48 <> 49

50 You drop a strategy question in as plain prose. Hivemind reads it for 51 the entities and decisions involved and breaks it into smaller 52 sub-claims you can actually evaluate one at a time. 53

54

55 You attach evidence to each branch — citations weighted by how 56 trustworthy and how recent the source is. Underneath, the software 57 keeps a running confidence number for every claim and rolls those up 58 to the question at the top. Above, you get a working surface: a tree 59 you can walk, edit, and challenge. 60

61

62 The output is a structured brief with citations and confidence 63 intervals — not a chat transcript. The reasoning travels with the 64 conclusion. 65

66 67 ), 68 }, 69 { 70 id: "status", 71 heading: "Where it sits", 72 body: ( 73 <> 74

75 Hivemind is in active development at seed stage. Question 76 decomposition, the evidence ledger, and the end-to-end workflow are 77 usable today. Scenario branching and counterfactual simulation are in 78 alpha against a small set of operator partners. Adversarial review and 79 the final briefing render are scoped but not yet shipping. 80

81

82 For deeper questions, ask the assistant on /chat, 83 or keep reading the project page below for the full breakdown. 84

85 86 ), 87 }, 88 ]; 89 90 // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P02 §B1). 91 if (hvmPhases.length !== 4) { 92 throw new Error( 93 `hvm.tsx: hvmPhases.length must be 4 (got ${hvmPhases.length})`, 94 ); 95 } 96

file.content:
import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; export const hvmPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "what", heading: "What Hivemind is", body: ( <>

Hivemind is strategic analytical software for operators reasoning under uncertainty — founders, analysts, and decision-makers who have to commit to a course of action when the evidence is incomplete and the stakes are real.

The one-line pitch: instead of a memo full of confident-sounding prose, you get a structured argument you can defend, attack, and update — with the assumptions named and the evidence attached.

), }, { id: "problem", heading: "What problem it solves", body: ( <>

Most strategic deliberation fails the same way. A team writes a memo, fluent prose hides the load-bearing assumptions, and nobody can argue with it because there is nothing concrete to argue against. The team commits anyway, and when reality disagrees, no one can point at the step where the reasoning broke.

Hivemind targets that failure. The working artifact is not a paragraph — it is a tree of claims with sources attached and a confidence number on each. When the answer changes, you can point at the claim that moved, and so can the next person who picks up the file.

), }, { id: "how", heading: "How it works, briefly", body: ( <>

You drop a strategy question in as plain prose. Hivemind reads it for the entities and decisions involved and breaks it into smaller sub-claims you can actually evaluate one at a time.

You attach evidence to each branch — citations weighted by how trustworthy and how recent the source is. Underneath, the software keeps a running confidence number for every claim and rolls those up to the question at the top. Above, you get a working surface: a tree you can walk, edit, and challenge.

The output is a structured brief with citations and confidence intervals — not a chat transcript. The reasoning travels with the conclusion.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Where it sits", body: ( <>

Hivemind is in active development at seed stage. Question decomposition, the evidence ledger, and the end-to-end workflow are usable today. Scenario branching and counterfactual simulation are in alpha against a small set of operator partners. Adversarial review and the final briefing render are scoped but not yet shipping.

For deeper questions, ask the assistant on /chat, or keep reading the project page below for the full breakdown.

), }, ]; // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P02 §B1). if (hvmPhases.length !== 4) { throw new Error( `hvm.tsx: hvmPhases.length must be 4 (got ${hvmPhases.length})`, ); }

content.content:
1 import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; 2 3 // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): 4 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/preface.ts 5 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/pillars.ts 6 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/arc.ts 7 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/progress.ts 8 // - src/content/projects/purposeless-efficiency/quotes.ts 9 10 export const prpPhases: Phase[] = [ 11 { 12 id: "distinction", 13 heading: "The distinction", 14 body: ( 15 <> 16

17 Purposeless efficiency is efficiency severed from any external 18 purpose, until the metric is the meaning. One concrete example: a 19 workflow that has been turned into a leaderboard, where the 20 leaderboard becomes the workflow and the worldview follows. 21

22

23 Purposeful efficiency still remembers what it is for; it optimises in 24 service of something outside itself. One concrete example: an 25 institution whose primary product remains the goods or services it 26 was built to deliver, rather than the legitimation of its own 27 continued existence. 28

29 30 ), 31 }, 32 { 33 id: "why-it-matters", 34 heading: "Why it matters", 35 body: ( 36 <> 37

38 When purposeless efficiency goes unrecognised, the optimization 39 target eats the optimized object. The first case is gamification: 40 every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the 41 workflow, and the institution survives while the thing it was 42 supposed to do does not. 43

44

45 The second case is incumbency and the complacency that follows from 46 it. Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they 47 are wired into the substrate of decision-making. Given levers 48 disconnected from outcomes, the rational response is to stop 49 pulling — the cost of paying attention exceeds the expected return, 50 the citizen withdraws, and so, eventually, does the elite. 51

52 53 ), 54 }, 55 { 56 id: "manuscript", 57 heading: "What I'm doing about it", 58 body: ( 59 <> 60

61 I'm writing a manuscript called Purposeless Efficiency — Book I of a 62 planned five-volume series. Book I is the diagnostic volume. It 63 opens with a refusal and a confession — that I was good at producing 64 inside systems whose only consistent output was their own 65 perpetuation — and then walks five pillars in turn: corporatism, 66 gamification, incumbency, complacency, and economic revolution. 67

68

69 The remaining four volumes will treat what comes after the 70 severance: the institutions that survive it, the ones that do not, 71 and what an honest economic order might look like once we admit the 72 mistake. Working titles for Books II through V are provisional; the 73 volumes are scoped at the volume level and remain in planning, not 74 drafting. 75

76 77 ), 78 }, 79 { 80 id: "status", 81 heading: "Status & where to go", 82 body: ( 83 <> 84

85 Book I is in active writing on manuscript version four, at roughly 86 92,000 of a target 100,000 words. Books II through V remain in 87 planning. There is no public release date and no publisher 88 announcement; this page exists to make the argument legible while 89 the manuscript continues. 90

91

92 For the longer breakdown, keep reading the project page below. For 93 questions, ask the assistant on /chat. 94

95 96 ), 97 }, 98 ]; 99 100 // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P03 §B1). 101 if (prpPhases.length !== 4) { 102 throw new Error( 103 `prp.tsx: prpPhases.length must be 4 (got ${prpPhases.length})`, 104 ); 105 } 106

Purposeless efficiency is efficiency severed from any external purpose, until the metric is the meaning. One concrete example: a workflow that has been turned into a leaderboard, where the leaderboard becomes the workflow and the worldview follows.

Purposeful efficiency still remembers what it is for; it optimises in service of something outside itself. One concrete example: an institution whose primary product remains the goods or services it was built to deliver, rather than the legitimation of its own continued existence.

), }, { id: "why-it-matters", heading: "Why it matters", body: ( <>

When purposeless efficiency goes unrecognised, the optimization target eats the optimized object. The first case is gamification: every workflow becomes a leaderboard, the leaderboard becomes the workflow, and the institution survives while the thing it was supposed to do does not.

The second case is incumbency and the complacency that follows from it. Incumbents persist not because they are right but because they are wired into the substrate of decision-making. Given levers disconnected from outcomes, the rational response is to stop pulling — the cost of paying attention exceeds the expected return, the citizen withdraws, and so, eventually, does the elite.

), }, { id: "manuscript", heading: "What I'm doing about it", body: ( <>

I'm writing a manuscript called Purposeless Efficiency — Book I of a planned five-volume series. Book I is the diagnostic volume. It opens with a refusal and a confession — that I was good at producing inside systems whose only consistent output was their own perpetuation — and then walks five pillars in turn: corporatism, gamification, incumbency, complacency, and economic revolution.

The remaining four volumes will treat what comes after the severance: the institutions that survive it, the ones that do not, and what an honest economic order might look like once we admit the mistake. Working titles for Books II through V are provisional; the volumes are scoped at the volume level and remain in planning, not drafting.

Book I is in active writing on manuscript version four, at roughly 92,000 of a target 100,000 words. Books II through V remain in planning. There is no public release date and no publisher announcement; this page exists to make the argument legible while the manuscript continues.

For the longer breakdown, keep reading the project page below. For questions, ask the assistant on /chat.

content.content:
1	// Prose narrative for the THS project page.
2	// Sources (every named principle and named example grounded in repo content):
3	//   - thesis.ts         → claim, method, role
4	//   - methodology.ts    → extraction with provenance; Noosphere walking the
5	//                          graph at intervals; not-a-chatbot framing
6	//   - principles.ts     → P-001, P-002, P-014, P-015, P-022, P-031, P-040,
7	//                          P-052, P-061, P-070, P-071, P-080 with their
8	//                          stated supports / tensions
9	//   - noosphere-feed.ts → the three feed kinds (DETECTED, OPEN QUESTION,
10	//                          REFINED) and the worked example of P-014 vs P-071
11	// The prompt asks the page to answer "what does the Theseus project argue
12	// about identity?". Theseus does not argue a substantive claim about
13	// identity; it argues that the question of which commitments survive
14	// revision is decidable by structure, and builds the system to make that
15	// structure visible. The body holds that line.
16	
17	export type BodySection = {
18	  slug: string;
19	  heading: string;
20	  paragraphs: string[];
21	};
22	
23	const sections: BodySection[] = [
24	  {
25	    slug: "the-ship-of-theseus-briefly",
26	    heading: "The Ship of Theseus, briefly",
27	    paragraphs: [
28	      "The puzzle in the name is the old one: a ship has each of its planks replaced over time, and at the end of the process there is a question about whether it is still the same ship. The puzzle has had a long career as a thought experiment about identity, but the project does not borrow it for the metaphysics. It borrows it for the method. The interesting question is not whether the ship is the same ship; it is which planks, when swapped, force you to admit that something has changed.",
29	      "Theseus, the project, is a knowledge system for keeping that ledger honestly. It extracts first principles from discussion and writing, links them into a graph of supports and tensions, and runs a second model — Noosphere — across the graph to flag contradictions, ask refining questions, and propose edits. The thesis is plain: philosophical debate can be systematized. The role is a Kim or a Codex for the operator-philosopher.",
30	    ],
31	  },
32	  {
33	    slug: "three-orders-of-identity-change",
34	    heading: "Three orders of identity change",
35	    paragraphs: [
36	      "The system tracks three orders of change to a principle, and each is a different answer to the Ship-of-Theseus question. The kinds come straight off the Noosphere feed and do most of the work the page is trying to make visible.",
37	      "First, DETECTED. Two principles you have already committed to are flagged as in tension — Noosphere notices, for example, that P-014 (\"autonomy is prior to welfare\") tenses with P-071 (\"collective survival overrides individual consent in extremis\"). Nothing has been replaced yet. The ship is intact, and an inspector has tapped one of the planks and said: this one, here, is not what you think it is.",
38	      "Second, OPEN QUESTION. The contradiction is not silently absorbed. It is rendered as a question that has to be answered before the graph is allowed to settle: under what conditions does P-014 yield to P-071, and does that yield imply a new meta-principle? The system refuses to choose for you. It only insists that the choice exists.",
39	      "Third, REFINED. A principle is rewritten in light of pressure from its neighbours — P-022, for instance, becomes \"preferences over outcomes are revealed through choice under constraint.\" The plank is replaced. The provenance of the old version is kept. Whether you call the resulting ship the same ship is your problem; whether the rewrite is consistent with the rest of the graph is the system's.",
40	    ],
41	  },
42	  {
43	    slug: "severity",
44	    heading: "Severity",
45	    paragraphs: [
46	      "Severity is the question of how much a contradiction is allowed to cost before it forces a change. Theseus reads it off the structure of the graph rather than off the heat of the argument. A tension between two principles that each support nothing else is a local quarrel. A tension whose endpoints are load-bearing — P-014 supports P-015 (\"consent is the operator of legitimacy\"), and both of them sit in tension with P-071 — propagates. The replacement of a plank near the keel is not the same as the replacement of a plank on the deck.",
47	      "The seed dataset deliberately includes a few of these load-bearing edges so the severity reading has something to grade. P-022 (\"preferences over outcomes are revealed through choice under constraint\") and P-031 (\"stated preferences and revealed preferences diverge predictably\") are stated as a tension on purpose: they are both true in their domains, and Theseus is meant to surface that the cost of holding both is the requirement to specify which domain you are in when you appeal to either.",
48	    ],
49	  },
50	  {
51	    slug: "refusal-cases",
52	    heading: "Refusal cases",
53	    paragraphs: [
54	      "Theseus is allowed, and required, to refuse. The refusal cases are the principles whose function in the graph is to bound what the system itself is willing to do. P-080 (\"a model that cannot be wrong cannot be useful\") is the cleanest example: it forbids any version of Theseus that quietly hides its own contradictions in order to look coherent. P-001 (\"knowledge is provisional; revision is a feature, not a flaw\") sits behind it and refuses, in the other direction, the temptation to treat any current state of the graph as final.",
55	      "The OPEN QUESTION feed kind is the operational shape of refusal. When a contradiction is detected and no resolution is yet defensible, Noosphere does not pick. It writes the question and waits. The slow, structured collaborator described in the methodology depends on this — it is what distinguishes the system from a chatbot that always has a take.",
56	    ],
57	  },
58	  {
59	    slug: "fixed-points",
60	    heading: "Fixed points",
61	    paragraphs: [
62	      "Fixed points are the principles a graph keeps coming back to under revision — the planks that survive every pass of the inspector. In the seed dataset they are the ones with no recorded tensions: P-001 on revisability, P-002 (\"evidence is weighed, not counted\"), P-040 (\"power flows toward whatever is measured\"), P-052 (\"metrics, once made consequential, cease to be informative\"), P-061 (\"institutions outlive the problems they were designed to solve\"), and P-070 (\"replacement is harder than reform; reform is harder than capture\"). Their stability is not a guarantee that they are right; it is a record that they have not yet been forced into a contradiction by anything else in the graph.",
63	      "Fixed points are the project's answer to the original puzzle. Identity, for Theseus, is not the persistence of the planks but the persistence of the constraints the planks have to satisfy. A position changes when a fixed point falls. Until then, the ship is still the ship.",
64	    ],
65	  },
66	  {
67	    slug: "status",
68	    heading: "Status",
69	    paragraphs: [
70	      "Theseus is live. The current seed dataset is twelve principles, with provenance carried alongside each one and a small Noosphere feed of detections, open questions, and refinements. The intended next step is to replace the seed set with the working corpus extracted from thesescodex.com once that API is available. The page exists to make the argument legible while the dataset grows; the system itself is the argument.",
71	    ],
72	  },
73	];
74	
75	export default sections;
76

1	// TODO(michael): rewrite in your voice; keep under 240 words
2	const methodology = [
3	  "Theseus extracts first principles from discussions, uploaded essays, and interviews using a language model fine-tuned on Michael's own working corpus. Each principle is stored with its provenance — the source text and the moment of extraction.",
4	  "Principles are linked into a graph of supports and tensions. A second model — Noosphere — walks this graph at intervals, flagging contradictions, asking refining questions, and proposing edits to bring sibling principles into coherence.",
5	  "The system is not a chatbot. It is a slow, structured collaborator for the operator-philosopher who wants to know, at any given moment, where their stated commitments contradict themselves.",
6	];
7	export default methodology;
8

1	import type { ProjectMetadata } from "@/lib/projects/types";
2	
3	// Tagline + framing sourced from existing repo content:
4	//   - thesis.ts      → claim ("philosophical debate can be systematized"),
5	//                       method (extract first principles, graph supports/
6	//                       tensions, surface contradictions), role
7	//                       ("a Kim/Codex for the operator-philosopher")
8	//   - methodology.ts → extraction with provenance, Noosphere walking the
9	//                       graph, slow structured collaborator (not a chatbot)
10	//   - principles.ts  → the seed dataset (P-001..P-080) with explicit
11	//                       supports / tensions edges
12	// The intended src/lib/theseus/* files (ordersDeck.ts, thesisAxes.ts,
13	// sources.ts) referenced by the prompt do not currently exist in the repo;
14	// the framing therefore stays grounded in the content/ files above.
15	export const metadata: ProjectMetadata = {
16	  slug: "theseus",
17	  code: "THS",
18	  title: "Theseus",
19	  tagline:
20	    "A knowledge system for monitoring contradiction in the principles you have already committed to.",
21	  status: "shipped",
22	  framing:
23	    "Theseus extracts first principles from discussion and writing, links them into a graph of supports and tensions, and runs a second model — Noosphere — across the graph to flag contradictions, ask refining questions, and propose edits. The product is a slow, structured collaborator for the operator-philosopher who wants to know, at any given moment, where their stated commitments contradict themselves. The output is a contradiction map with provenance, not a debate.",
24	  externalLinks: [
25	    { label: "thesescodex.com", href: "https://thesescodex.com" },
26	  ],
27	  seedQuestion: "what does the Theseus project argue about identity?",
28	};
29	
30	export default metadata;
31

content.text: I have the context I need. Let me write the THS presentation deck.

input.content:
import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): // - src/content/projects/theseus/thesis.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/methodology.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/principles.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/body.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/metadata.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/noosphere-feed.ts // Per 17/P04 §D: the three orders of change are NOT named individually // here. The project page (16/P06) handles that depth. This deck is // surface-level: orders of change, severity, fixed points. export const thsPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "puzzle", heading: "The Ship of Theseus, plainly", body: ( <>

A ship has each of its planks replaced over time. At the end of the process, every original board is gone. The question is whether it is still the same ship. That is the puzzle — a thought experiment about identity that has been turned over since antiquity, with no settled answer.

The puzzle survives because the question is not really about ships. It is about anything that changes while staying recognisably itself: a person, a company, a body of beliefs. If you cannot say which changes preserve the thing and which replace it, you cannot say what the thing is. The interesting move is to stop asking whether the ship is the same ship and start asking which planks, when swapped, force you to admit something has changed.

), }, { id: "frame", heading: "The frame I'm using", body: ( <>

The project's frame for identity-under-change has three parts. The first is that not every change is the same change — there are different orders of revision, and a system honest about identity has to distinguish a tap on the hull from a plank pulled near the keel. The second is severity: how much a contradiction is allowed to cost is read off the structure of what depends on what, not off the heat of the argument.

The third is fixed points — the commitments a position keeps coming back to under revision, the planks that survive every pass of the inspector. Identity, on this frame, is not the persistence of the planks but the persistence of the constraints they have to satisfy. A position changes when a fixed point falls. Until then, the ship is still the ship.

), }, { id: "project", heading: "What's in the project", body: ( <>

Theseus is the working out of that frame as a system. It extracts first principles from discussion and writing, stores each with its provenance, and links them into a graph of supports and tensions. A second model — Noosphere — walks the graph at intervals, flagging contradictions, asking refining questions, and proposing edits. The thesis is plain: philosophical debate can be systematized.

The seed dataset is twelve principles with supports and tensions wired between them, including a few load-bearing edges so the severity reading has something to grade. The methodology and the principles list are on the project page below, along with worked examples of how flagged contradictions become open questions and, eventually, refined principles.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Status & where to go", body: ( <>

Theseus is live. The current dataset is twelve seed principles with provenance and a small Noosphere feed; the next step is to replace the seed set with the working corpus extracted from thesescodex.com. The full breakdown — orders of change, severity, refusal cases, fixed points — is on the project page below.

For questions, ask the assistant on /chat.

), }, ]; // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P04 §B1). if (thsPhases.length !== 4) { throw new Error( `ths.tsx: thsPhases.length must be 4 (got ${thsPhases.length})`, ); }

The project page (16/P06) handles that depth. This deck is // surface-level: orders of change, severity, fixed points. export const thsPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "puzzle", heading: "The Ship of Theseus, plainly", body: ( <>

A ship has each of its planks replaced over time. At the end of the process, every original board is gone. The question is whether it is still the same ship. That is the puzzle — a thought experiment about identity that has been turned over since antiquity, with no settled answer.

The puzzle survives because the question is not really about ships. It is about anything that changes while staying recognisably itself: a person, a company, a body of beliefs. If you cannot say which changes preserve the thing and which replace it, you cannot say what the thing is. The interesting move is to stop asking whether the ship is the same ship and start asking which planks, when swapped, force you to admit something has changed.

), }, { id: "frame", heading: "The frame I'm using", body: ( <>

The project's frame for identity-under-change has three parts. The first is that not every change is the same change — there are different orders of revision, and a system honest about identity has to distinguish a tap on the hull from a plank pulled near the keel. The second is severity: how much a contradiction is allowed to cost is read off the structure of what depends on what, not off the heat of the argument.

The third is fixed points — the commitments a position keeps coming back to under revision, the planks that survive every pass of the inspector. Identity, on this frame, is not the persistence of the planks but the persistence of the constraints they have to satisfy. A position changes when a fixed point falls. Until then, the ship is still the ship.

), }, { id: "project", heading: "What's in the project", body: ( <>

Theseus is the working out of that frame as a system. It extracts first principles from discussion and writing, stores each with its provenance, and links them into a graph of supports and tensions. A second model — Noosphere — walks the graph at intervals, flagging contradictions, asking refining questions, and proposing edits. The thesis is plain: philosophical debate can be systematized.

The seed dataset is twelve principles with supports and tensions wired between them, including a few load-bearing edges so the severity reading has something to grade. The methodology and the principles list are on the project page below, along with worked examples of how flagged contradictions become open questions and, eventually, refined principles.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Status & where to go", body: ( <>

Theseus is live. The current dataset is twelve seed principles with provenance and a small Noosphere feed; the next step is to replace the seed set with the working corpus extracted from thesescodex.com. The full breakdown — orders of change, severity, refusal cases, fixed points — is on the project page below.

For questions, ask the assistant on /chat.

import type { Phase } from "../../components/presentation/types"; // Sources (every claim grounded in existing repo content): // - src/content/projects/theseus/thesis.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/methodology.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/principles.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/body.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/metadata.ts // - src/content/projects/theseus/noosphere-feed.ts // Per 17/P04 §D: the three orders of change are NOT named individually // here. The project page (16/P06) handles that depth. This deck is // surface-level: orders of change, severity, fixed points. export const thsPhases: Phase[] = [ { id: "puzzle", heading: "The Ship of Theseus, plainly", body: ( <>

A ship has each of its planks replaced over time. At the end of the process, every original board is gone. The question is whether it is still the same ship. That is the puzzle — a thought experiment about identity that has been turned over since antiquity, with no settled answer.

The puzzle survives because the question is not really about ships. It is about anything that changes while staying recognisably itself: a person, a company, a body of beliefs. If you cannot say which changes preserve the thing and which replace it, you cannot say what the thing is. The interesting move is to stop asking whether the ship is the same ship and start asking which planks, when swapped, force you to admit something has changed.

), }, { id: "frame", heading: "The frame I'm using", body: ( <>

The project's frame for identity-under-change has three parts. The first is that not every change is the same change — there are different orders of revision, and a system honest about identity has to distinguish a tap on the hull from a plank pulled near the keel. The second is severity: how much a contradiction is allowed to cost is read off the structure of what depends on what, not off the heat of the argument.

The third is fixed points — the commitments a position keeps coming back to under revision, the planks that survive every pass of the inspector. Identity, on this frame, is not the persistence of the planks but the persistence of the constraints they have to satisfy. A position changes when a fixed point falls. Until then, the ship is still the ship.

), }, { id: "project", heading: "What's in the project", body: ( <>

Theseus is the working out of that frame as a system. It extracts first principles from discussion and writing, stores each with its provenance, and links them into a graph of supports and tensions. A second model — Noosphere — walks the graph at intervals, flagging contradictions, asking refining questions, and proposing edits. The thesis is plain: philosophical debate can be systematized.

The seed dataset is twelve principles with supports and tensions wired between them, including a few load-bearing edges so the severity reading has something to grade. The methodology and the principles list are on the project page below, along with worked examples of how flagged contradictions become open questions and, eventually, refined principles.

), }, { id: "status", heading: "Status & where to go", body: ( <>

Theseus is live. The current dataset is twelve seed principles with provenance and a small Noosphere feed; the next step is to replace the seed set with the working corpus extracted from thesescodex.com. The full breakdown — orders of change, severity, refusal cases, fixed points — is on the project page below.

For questions, ask the assistant on /chat.

), }, ]; // Invariant: exactly 4 phases (per 17/P04 §B1). if (thsPhases.length !== 4) { throw new Error( `ths.tsx: thsPhases.length must be 4 (got ${thsPhases.length})`, ); }
